description: Following the February 2014 collapse of the Tokyo-based Bitcoin exchange Mt. Gox, which lost 850,000 BTC, global financial regulators entered a heated debate regarding whether to treat cryptocurrency exchanges as traditional financial institutions. The event polarized public opinion between libertarian crypto-enthusiasts advocating for autonomy and mainstream investors demanding strict oversight.

key_events.description: Mt. Gox suspends all trading and declares bankruptcy after revealing the loss of customer funds.
Japanese government officials state that Bitcoin is not a currency and regulators lack the mandate to protect users.
US Senate Committee on Homeland Security holds hearings on the dangers of illicit activities on crypto platforms.
The New York Department of Financial Services announces plans for the 'BitLicense' framework.

calibration_notes: This scenario provides a baseline for how a single catastrophic failure of a market-leading entity shifts public opinion from 'techno-optimism' to 'regulatory necessity' during the early adoption phase of an asset class.
